text: Mixed municipal solid waste appears as a heterogeneous heap of small, varied items with no dominant colour: torn plastic bags, food scraps, paper, packaging, and household odds and ends tangled together. From above it reads as a mottled, speckled patch of browns, whites, and bright plastic accents, often loose and wind-scattered around its edges. It rarely has clean geometry; the pile is lumpy and irregular, spilling outward rather than stacked.

Construction and demolition waste looks like a coarse grey-to-buff pile of broken concrete, shattered brick, chunks of mortar, and bent rebar poking out at angles. The material is blocky and angular, dusty, and often mixed with splintered timber, cracked tiles, and twisted metal. Seen from a distance it forms a pale, rubble-textured mound distinct from soil by its sharp edges and uniform dusty tone.

Concrete rubble is a pile of hard grey fragments ranging from fist-sized chunks to large slabs, with rough fractured faces and occasional protruding steel reinforcement. The colour is a flat, dusty grey, sometimes streaked with the buff of attached mortar. The blocks stack unevenly with dark voids between them and a fine pale dust coating the surface.

Waste bricks and masonry show as stacks or scattered piles of rectangular red-brown and orange blocks, many broken into halves or corners, crusted with grey mortar. The regular block shape and warm terracotta colour set them apart from grey concrete. Weathered piles fade to a dull, dusty ochre.

Milled asphalt and road-surfacing waste looks like coarse black-to-charcoal gravel or broken slabs with a dull matte finish. Fresh material is almost black; weathered pieces grey slightly and may show embedded pale aggregate stones. It piles in loose granular heaps or stacked flat plates with sharp broken edges.

Waste tyres are black rings of uniform size, either stacked in neat columns, laid flat in overlapping circles, or dumped in a tangled dark mass. Their consistent circular outline and deep matte black make them one of the most recognisable waste types from the air, forming a pattern of repeated dark doughnut shapes. Piles look bulky yet regular, with round holes casting shadows.

End-of-life vehicles appear as recognisable car and van shells, often rusted, dented, missing wheels, doors, or glass, sometimes stacked or crushed into flattened cuboids. Colours are faded paint over orange rust streaks. In scrapyards they line up in rows or pile into layered stacks of crushed metal boxes with the vague silhouette of a vehicle still visible.

Scrap metal is a jumbled mass of silvery, rusty-orange, and dull-grey pieces: bent sheets, pipes, wire, rods, and machine parts with a hard reflective sheen where fresh and a red-brown crust where corroded. The pile bristles with sharp protruding edges and tangled linear forms. Sunlight glints off the metallic faces, giving it a patchy bright-and-dark texture unlike organic waste.

Mixed plastic waste is a bright, chaotic jumble of bottles, tubs, film, and fragments in saturated colours — whites, blues, reds, greens — with a glossy or crinkled surface. It is lightweight and easily wind-blown, so it scatters and drapes over surrounding ground and vegetation. From above it reads as a vivid, high-contrast speckle standing out sharply against natural earth tones.

Agricultural plastic film and silage wrap appears as large sheets and rolls of black, white, or translucent plastic, often torn, muddy, and half-buried at field edges. Discarded it billows and folds into crumpled drifts that catch on fences and hedgerows. Wet and weathered, it dulls to a dirty grey-white with a flapping, sheet-like form.

Green waste is a mound of leaves, branches, grass clippings, and hedge trimmings, vivid green when fresh and fading to yellow-brown and dark compost tones as it decays. The texture is fibrous and tangled with protruding twigs and stems. Piles slump and flatten over time, blending toward the surrounding vegetation in colour but distinguishable by their heaped, cut form.

Food and organic waste appears as a wet, lumpy mass of decomposing matter in mottled browns, dark greens, and greyish rot, often glistening with moisture. It slumps and spreads rather than holding shape, attracts birds and flies, and darkens as it breaks down. Fresh additions show recognisable fruit, vegetable, and food fragments before they blacken.

Wood waste is a pile of pale-brown planks, offcuts, pallets, branches, and splintered timber, often mixed with protruding nails and painted fragments. The linear plank shapes and warm tan-to-grey tones give it a stacked, criss-crossed appearance. Weathered wood greys and silvers; treated or painted pieces add flecks of colour.

Paper and cardboard waste shows as bales, boxes, and loose sheets in buff-brown and off-white, with flat folded forms and crushed corrugated edges. Dry, it is light and lifts in the wind; wet, it darkens to soggy grey-brown and mats down. Baled cardboard forms tidy rectangular blocks, while loose paper scatters as a pale litter.

Glass waste (cullet) is a heap of angular translucent fragments in clear, green, and amber, with sharp glinting edges that catch and scatter light. Crushed fine, it reads as a glittering gravel; in larger pieces, broken bottles and jars remain identifiable. Its reflective sparkle and cool colours distinguish it from opaque rubble.

Electronic waste is a jumble of casings, circuit boards, cables, screens, and small appliances in greys, blacks, and beige plastics, with green circuit boards and tangled multicoloured wiring. The forms are boxy and man-made, mixing dull plastic housings with glinting metal contacts. Piles look cluttered and angular, speckled with the distinctive green of exposed boards.

Discarded white goods — fridges, washing machines, ovens — appear as large boxy white, cream, or metallic units, often dented and rust-streaked, dumped singly or stacked. Their clean rectangular shape and pale colour make them stand out sharply against ground and vegetation. Age brings yellowing plastic and orange rust at the seams.

Hazardous chemical drums and containers are cylindrical steel or plastic barrels, typically blue, black, or rusted metal, often bulging, dented, or leaking dark stains onto the ground around them. Grouped, they form clusters of upright or toppled cylinders. Corroded drums show streaks of rust and pooled residues, and warning labels or discolouration hint at their contents.

Asbestos-cement sheeting appears as flat, corrugated or plain grey-white panels, often broken into rigid slabs with crumbling, fibrous edges. The dull chalky grey and rectangular profiled shape resemble old roofing. Weathered pieces show moss staining and cracked, powdery breaks, and they lie stacked or scattered as brittle boards.

Waste insulation shows as fluffy or rigid slabs of mineral wool and foam boards in pale yellow, pink, white, and grey, light and easily crumbled. Torn batts fray into fibrous clumps that catch the wind, while foam boards snap into clean rectangular pieces shedding beads. The soft, matte, low-density mass contrasts with dense rubble.

Plasterboard waste is a stack of flat white-to-grey panels with a paper-faced surface and a chalky gypsum core that crumbles at broken edges. Snapped boards show a clean powdery white interior. Wet, it sags and disintegrates into a soft pale paste, distinct from the hard grey of concrete.

Excavated soil and spoil appears as bare mounds of brown-to-ochre earth, sometimes mixed with stones, clay lumps, and root fragments, with a smooth or clumpy texture. Fresh heaps are dark and damp; dried surfaces pale and crack. Large spoil heaps have a raw, unvegetated look that stands out against surrounding greenery.

Mining tailings form broad flat or terraced expanses of fine grey, ochre, or reddish sediment, often ponded with discoloured water or crusted and cracked when dry. The material is powdery and uniform, spreading in vast pale sheets that contrast sharply with natural terrain. Colours can be unnaturally rust-red or chalky from mineral content.

Incinerator bottom ash and slag is a grey-to-black granular pile of clinker, fused lumps, and fine ash flecked with glassy and metallic fragments. The tone is dark and dusty, coarser than soil, sometimes streaked with rust from embedded metal. It heaps in dull charcoal mounds with a gritty, cindery texture.

Sludge appears as a wet, dark, semi-liquid mass in greys, browns, and blacks, glistening and slumping to fill any depression. It has a smooth, mud-like or paste texture, sometimes cracked into plates where the surface has dried. Its glossy dark sheen and formless spread distinguish it from dry soil.

Tar and bituminous waste is a glossy black, sticky mass that pools and hardens into shiny dark lumps and sheets. Fresh, it looks wet and reflective; set, it becomes a dull matte black brittle crust. Dark stains typically spread into the ground around it.

Textile waste is a soft tangled heap of clothing, fabric, and rags in every colour, faded and often mud-stained, with limp draping folds. It slumps and mats down when wet and scatters loosely when dry. From above it reads as a muted multicoloured patch with irregular soft edges.

Dumped mattresses and bulky furniture appear as large flat or boxy items — striped or stained mattress slabs, broken sofas, chairs, and cabinets — with recognisable everyday shapes. Faded fabric, exposed foam, and splintered frames mark their decay. Their size and familiar silhouettes make them conspicuous against open ground.

A fly-tipped dump is a mixed pile at a roadside, gateway, or lay-by, combining bin bags, furniture, appliances, rubble, and loose litter in a chaotic low mound. It spills from the verge onto the road or field edge, colours clashing and forms jumbled. The tell-tale signs are its abrupt placement at an access point and the trail of scattered debris around it.

Burnt waste residue is a blackened, ashy patch of charred fragments, melted plastic blobs, twisted metal, and grey ash, ringed by scorched ground. The dominant tone is sooty black and pale ash-grey, with the underlying vegetation seared brown. Partially burned items retain warped shapes amid the ash.

A tyre stockpile is a massive accumulation of black tyres heaped into a dark mound or spread across a lot in repeating circular forms. The uniform matte-black rings and their sheer number create a distinctive textured dark expanse. Edges slump into loose rolling tyres, and the pile casts a pattern of round shadows.

Discarded oil and fuel containers are jerricans, cans, and small drums in weathered plastic and metal — blues, whites, blacks, and rust — often crushed, leaking, and surrounded by dark oily stains. They cluster in small heaps or scatter across a site. Sheens and dark ground patches signal spilled contents.

Clinical waste appears as yellow or orange bags and rigid sharps bins, plus tubing, dressings, and gloves, the strong warning colours standing out sharply. Bags are glossy and sealed; spilled contents show white and translucent plastic items. The vivid yellow-orange coding is the clearest visual marker of this waste.

Waste batteries are small cylindrical and rectangular units in metallic silver, black, and coloured casings, sometimes corroded with white or greenish crust at the terminals. Heaped, they form a dense metallic-and-plastic speckle. Larger lead-acid batteries appear as dark boxy blocks, often cracked and leaking.

Waste fluorescent tubes are long slender white glass cylinders, easily broken into curved shards, mixed with the bulbous shapes of spent lamps. Intact tubes lie in pale linear bundles; broken ones scatter as fine white glass. Their thin straight form and chalky white coating are distinctive.

Paint and varnish waste is a cluster of metal and plastic tins, often dented and rusted, with dried drips and splashes of bright colour down their sides and pooled on the ground. Open or crushed cans reveal hardened skins of colour. Multicoloured stains around the containers are a clear sign.

Animal carcass and slaughter waste appears as pale-to-reddish fleshy masses, hides, bones, and offal, wet and darkening as they decompose. The forms are soft and organic, attracting birds and insects, with dark staining spreading beneath. Colours shift from pink-red to grey-green rot.

Scattered litter is a thin, dispersed scatter of small bright items — wrappers, cups, cans, bottles, and bags — spread loosely across ground, verges, and gutters rather than heaped. Individual pieces are tiny but their colours pop against pavement and grass. Wind gathers them against kerbs, fences, and vegetation.

A mixed demolition pile is a large rubble mound blending broken concrete, brick, timber, twisted metal, wiring, and shattered fittings in a dusty grey-brown mass. Angular chunks and protruding beams give it a jagged skyline. Fine pale dust coats everything, and colour comes only from the odd painted board or plastic fragment.
